coredata.dc:title: Tailored nanoplatforms with detachable ‘meteorolite’ for photothermal-enhanced programmed tumor therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Qinfu
Zhang, Yunran
Yu, Tong
Lu, Junya
Sun, Guangxiang
Luo, Xinyi
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: The premature drug release, multidrug resistance, and limited tumor penetration ability have vastly weakened the chemotherapy effect. To surmount this dilemma, iRGD modified red-light emitting carbon dots (CDR) were gated on paclitaxel (PTX) loaded hollow mesoporous carbon (HMC) via cleavable disulfide bonds for photothermal-enhanced programmed tumor therapy. The HMC vehicles were designed based on their high photothermal conversion efficiency and tailored mesoporous structure with considerable drug loading capacity. The fluorescent CDR worked as the smart gatekeepers to prevent premature release, photothermal agents to enhance the photothermal effect of HMC, and the fluorescent agent to visualize the delivery process. The modification of iRGD peptide could promote the targeting and penetration capacities of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD. The tailored nanoplatforms could promptly divide into (1) unlocked PTX/HMC for synergistic thermo-chemotherapy on the surface of tumor cells, and (2) abundant CDR-iRGD as detachable ‘meteorolite’ with enhanced penetration ability to realize photothermal ablation at the deep-seated tumor. PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed outstanding synergistic cells killing efficiency of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy at different depths, exhibiting great potential in clinical application.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation process of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD nanoplatforms; (B) Schematic illustration of tailored nanoplatforms for photothermal-enhanced programmed tumor therapy at different depths. Once reaching the cytoplasm of tumor cells, the nanoplatforms could promptly divide into PTX/HMC and abundant CDR-iRGD with enhanced penetration ability. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The TEM images (A) and (B) of CDR; AFM pictures of CDR (C) AFM 3D image, (D) AFM topography picture, (E) Height profile along the line in (D); (F) Photoluminescent emission spectra of CDR at different excitation wavelengths; (insert: the photos of CDR under 365 nm UV and daylight illumination) (G) Excitation and emission spectra of CDR; (H) Photothermal heating profiles of CDR suspensions with series of concentrations under the 808 nm irradiation at 2 W/cm2 and (I) 200 μg/mL CDR under different power intensities; (J) Six cycles of NIR irradiation for CDR at 2 W/cm.2.

TEM images (A) of HMC-COOH, (B) HMC-CD-iRGD and (C) PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD; (D) Nitrogen isotherms and (E) pore size distributions of HMC-COOH, HMC-SS-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD; (F) Zeta potential changes after each modification (n = 3); (G) Particle size distributions and polydispersity index (PDI) of HMC-COOH, HMC-SS-CDR, HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD. (H) Normalized absorbances of HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD. (I) Hemolysis percentage of HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD (n = 3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The heating cycles of 808 nm irradiation of (A) HMC-COOH and (B) HMC-CD-iRGD suspensions at 1 W/cm2. Linear time data versus –lnθ from the cooling stage of (C) HMC-COOH and (D) HMC-CD-iRGD photothermal effect curve (illustration); (E) Photothermal heating curves of 4T1 cells incubated with HMC-CD-iRGD. (F) XRD and (G) DSC patterns of raw PTX, HMC-CD-iRGD, physical mixture (PM) and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD; (H) Drug release profiles of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD and free PTX in different release media; (I) Drug release profiles of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD under different conditions in pH 6.5 PBS. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) CLSM results of 4T1 cells preincubated with HMC-CD-iRGD with different irradiation time (1.0 W/cm2); (B) CLSM results for the cellular uptake of HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD; (C) FCM analysis of cellular uptake of HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD by 4T1 cells; (D) CLSM results of 4T1 cells incubated with free C6 and C6/HMC-CD-iRGD in the absence/presence of irradiation for 2 h; (E) FCM analysis of 4T1 cells incubated with free C6 and C6/HMC-CD-iRGD with or without irradiation. (Scale bar = 50 μm); The bio-TEM images of (F) blank 4T1 cells and (G) 4T1 cells after harvesting HMC-CD-iRGD for 4 h.

(A) The distribution of PTX/HMC-CD-PEG and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD in 4T1 MCTSs using CLSM examination. (Scale bar = 100 μm). (B) FCM analysis of 4T1 cells apoptosis after various treatments by Annexin V-FITC and PI staining; (C) CLSM of one-step TUNEL apoptosis of 4T1 cells with different treatments (DNase I was used as a positive group) (Scale bar = 20 μm). (D) The cell cycle distribution of 4T1 cells after different treatments. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) CLSM of 4T1 cells incubated with different groups; (B) CLSM images of MCTSs with different pretreatments; (C) Quantification of the percentage of living/dead cells. (D) The optical images of MCTSs incubated with various samples at different times (Group 1: Saline, Group 2: Taxol® Group 3: PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD, Group 4: HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR irradiation, Group 5: PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR irradiation, bar = 100 μm); The cell viabilities of 4T1 cells (E) without NIR or (F) with NIR irradiation (n = 5). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Fluorescence images of isolated tumors and main organs at 2, 6 and 24 h; (B) Fluorescence semi-quantification of Dir biodistributions (Dir: Ex = 720 nm, Em = 790 nm, n = 3); (C) Tumor growth curves of mice after different treatment groups; (D) Relative tumor mass (n = 5, *p < 0.05,***p < 0.001); (E) Body weight change curves and (F) images of excised tumors of mice (n = 5); (G) Tumor penetration of HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD in vivo with CDR as fluorescent probe (Scale bars = 200 μm); (H) TUNEL staining of tumor slides after treatment. (Scale bars = 100 μm).
IC50 values of HMC-CD-PEG, HMC-CD-iRGD, Taxol®, PTX/HMC-CD-PEG and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD towards 4T1 cells.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The IC50 value is equal to the concentration of PTX.

head.ce:title: Tailored nanoplatforms with detachable ‘meteorolite’ for photothermal-enhanced programmed tumor therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changzhou Pharmaceutical Factory Co. LTD, 518 East Laodong Road, Tianning District, Changzhou City, Jiangsu Province, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Changzhou Pharmaceutical Factory Co. LTD, 518 East Laodong Road, Tianning District, Changzhou City, Jiangsu Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The premature drug release, multidrug resistance, and limited tumor penetration ability have vastly weakened the chemotherapy effect. To surmount this dilemma, iRGD modified red-light emitting carbon dots (CDR) were gated on paclitaxel (PTX) loaded hollow mesoporous carbon (HMC) via cleavable disulfide bonds for photothermal-enhanced programmed tumor therapy. The HMC vehicles were designed based on their high photothermal conversion efficiency and tailored mesoporous structure with considerable drug loading capacity. The fluorescent CDR worked as the smart gatekeepers to prevent premature release, photothermal agents to enhance the photothermal effect of HMC, and the fluorescent agent to visualize the delivery process. The modification of iRGD peptide could promote the targeting and penetration capacities of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD. The tailored nanoplatforms could promptly divide into (1) unlocked PTX/HMC for synergistic thermo-chemotherapy on the surface of tumor cells, and (2) abundant CDR-iRGD as detachable ‘meteorolite’ with enhanced penetration ability to realize photothermal ablation at the deep-seated tumor. PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed outstanding synergistic cells killing efficiency of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy at different depths, exhibiting great potential in clinical application.

ce:section.ce:para: The American Cancer Society reported that in the past ten years, cancer has been showing a trend of younger age. The incidence of cancer among young people of all ages (15–39 years old) is rising increasingly. In 2020, there will be approximately 89500 new cancer cases aged 15–39 years in the United States [1]. At present, cancer has become a primary disease threatening human health worldwide. Chemotherapy is one of the most widely employed means to suppress tumor proliferation and prolong the span of patients [2]. However, conventional chemotherapy usually has low drug loading efficiency, premature release of the drug, and poor single treatment effect. In this case, tumor microenvironment (TME) responsive nano-materials have been developed in recent decades, owing to controlled and precise drug delivery in specific stimulation conditions [3,4].

Nowadays, mesoporous nanocarriers have obtained extraordinary breakthroughs because of the tunable pore size, huge surface area, and high porosity [5]. For instance, mesoporous silica carriers have been widely employed in drug delivery to enhance drug loading efficiency and reduce side effects. In addition to the advantages of other mesoporous materials, mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MCN), as a new class of mesoporous nanocarriers, have gained extensive attention due to their high loading and controlled release for aromatic drugs guaranteed by unique supramolecular π-π stacking as well as the extensive absorption in visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions [6,7]. Meanwhile, compared with MCN, HMC possesses lower density and higher loading capacity owing to the distinct hollow mesoporous structure [8], and has been used as an outstanding NIR adsorbing agent for photothermal therapy (PTT) recently [9–11]. NIR light (700–1200 nm) is the transparent “therapeutic window” of body tissues because of its profound penetration ability and minor damage to the human body [12]. In external NIR irradiation, the photothermal-adsorbing vehicles could convert light energy into poisonous heat in tumor tissue [13]. It is reported that the elevated temperature could promote cellular uptake of nanoparticles, chemotherapeutic drug release amount, and increase the sensitivity of tumor cells to chemotherapy [14]. Therefore, combining PTT and chemotherapy might play a synergistic effect to overcome the drawbacks of employing a single therapy.

Restricted penetration is another primary obstacle for nanoplatforms in the treatment of solid tumors, mainly owing to their disorganized vasculature, enhanced interstitial fluid pressure, and dense extracellular matrix in the TME of solid tumors [15–17]. To increase the tumor penetration depth, two main strategies, including penetrating peptide modification and particle size control, have been developed. Ultra-small nanovehicles benefit tumor penetration as the diffusion ability is oppositely related to particle size [18,19]. However, ultra-small nanoparticles were usually liable to quick clearance in circulation [20,21]. To tackle this predicament, size-changeable nanoparticles with larger initial particle sizes and longer half-life have been developed, which can promptly disassemble into ultra-small nanoparticles in tumor tissue for improved intra-tumoral diffusion [21,22]. Meanwhile, diverse peptides with distinct tumor targeting/penetrating functions were developed to mediate transcellular delivery and increase the penetration depth [23,24]. Both the strategies can increase the penetration depth of nanoplatforms, ultimately benefiting the desired therapeutic performance.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a new kind of photoluminescent nanoparticles, have caught worldwide attention because of their predominant merits, like excellent luminescence peculiarity [25], excellent biocompatibility [26], water dispersibility, high photostability, as well as facile modification and preparation. However, CDs still face some challenges. Most reported CDs only emit blue to green fluorescence, showing a weak light penetration capability [27,28]. So red-light emitting fluorescence CDs (CDR) NPs with good photothermal conversion efficiency are expected to provide a deeper light penetration and tumor-home PTT effect. Therefore, ultra-small nanoparticles with good photothermal conversion efficiency were considered to be gated on the opening of mesopores of HMC by cleaved bonds to hinder the premature drug release and realize the stimulus-responsive release. Then the detached ultra-small nanoparticles could penetrate into the depth of the tumor and realize the PTT-enhanced therapeutic effect.

Herein, a tailored nanoplatform was constructed based on CDR gated HMC via the disulfide bonds (HMC-CD-iRGD) for programmed tumor therapy as shown in Fig. 1 . The iRGD modified CDR (CDR-iRGD) achieved ‘one stone four birds’ functions including: (1) the gatekeepers to realize the dual-responsive drug release; (2) photothermal agents to further enhance the heat generation efficiency of HMC-CD-iRGD; (3) red-light emitting fluorescent tracker to visualize the delivery process; (4) detachable ‘meteorolite’ to accomplish photothermal ablation at the deep-seated tumor. The loading efficiency of poorly soluble drug paclitaxel (PTX) was 30.7% in HMC-CD-iRGD because of orderly mesoporous structure, tremendous surface area, and strong adsorption capability, which was higher than the reported drug loading efficiency for gold nanoparticles [29], carbon nanotubes [30], and iron oxide nanoparticles [31]. When the PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD reached the tumor sites, the nanoplatform could be uptaken by the tumor cells via the primary αvβ3 integrin receptor, followed by binding to the neuropilin-1 (Nrp-1) receptor on the surface of tumor cells to mediate the cell membrane penetration function. After entering the tumor cells, the nanoplatform could rapidly break into uncovered PTX/HMC and CDR-iRGD under the high concentration of GSH. The PTX/HMC realized the dual-responsive drug release and synergistic thermo-chemotherapy on the surface of tumor cells; and the highly penetrative ultra-small-sized CDR-iRGD could penetrate into the deep-seated malignant tumor to achieve NIR-triggered thermal ablation, which obviously improved the tumor penetration ability compared with the reported ultra-small nanoparticles gated mesoporous carrier delivery systems [32,33]. It was expected that after destroying the tumor cells at different depths through the programmed therapeutic patterns, PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD would observably inhibit the solid tumor growth under NIR irradiation. In conclusion, the tailored PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed a more inspiring inhibitory effect compared with the reported work based on mesoporous carbon vehicles [34], which was largely ascribed to the synergistic effect of penetrating peptide modification (iRGD) and size-changeable strategy (detachable ‘meteorolite’). However, there are still deficiencies need to be further improved. Due to the fluorescence quenching effect of carbon-based nanoparticles, the fluorescence intensity of CDR-iRGD was faint when being linked with HMC. And the fluorescence could be recovered after separation from HMC, which was not conducive to tracing the real-time in-vivo process.

Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 98%), hydrofluoric acid and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC, >99%), double-terminated carboxylated polyethylene glycol (HOOC-PEG-COOH, Mw = 2000), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), p-phenylenediamine, propidium iodide (PI), DiIC18(7)1,1′-dioctadecyltetramethylindotricarbocyanine Iodide (Dir), Calcein AM, 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), glutathione (GSH), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Inc. (Shanghai, China). Cell culture media RPMI-1640, penicillin-streptomycin, PTX and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were supplied by Dalian Meilun Biotech. Com. Ltd. (Dalian, China). All analytical reagents were not further purified before use.

CDR nanoparticles were prepared by a facile and green hydrothermal treatment process as follows [35]. Briefly, 0.11 g p-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 20 mL DMF solvent and placed in a hydrothermal autoclave, and further heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The obtained sample was dialyzed, freeze-dried and placed at 4 °C. The HMC was synthesized following our previous reports [32,33]. HMC-SS-COOH was synthesized based on a reported work [33]. The above as-synthesized CDR (2 mg/mL) was activated with NHS and EDC for 1 h in pH 7.4 PBS, and then 25 mg HMC-SS-COOH was added. After stirring for 24 h, the product was centrifuged to collect HMC-SS-CDR.

To prepare HMC-CD-PEG, 10 mg HOOC-PEG-COOH was dispersed in pH 5.8 PBS, followed by adding EDC and NHS for 1 h, and then 5 mg/mL HMC-SS-CDR was additionally added. After reacting for 12 h, HMC-CD-PEG was centrifuged and washed. Subsequently, 20 mg HMC-CD-PEG was activated with NHS and EDC for 1 h in pH 7.4 PBS, and then 5 mg iRGD was added. After 12 h, the HMC-CD-iRGD product was collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PTX loading process and stimuli-responsive drug release in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: PTX was used to study the stimuli-responsive release properties and thermochemotherapy of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD. The PTX was loaded in HMC-SS-COOH by the solvent evaporation method, followed by the capping of CDR. Briefly, 20 mg HMC-SS-COOH was dispersed in pH 7.4 PBS, and then 4 mL PTX acetonitrile solution (5 mg/mL) was added into the HMC-SS-COOH with continuous stirring for 36 h to evaporate the acetonitrile. Subsequently, the PTX loaded HMC-SS-COOH was further dispersed in pH 7.4 PBS and activated with EDC and NHS for 1 h, and then CDR was further added to be modified on the opening of HMC to prepare PTX loaded PTX/HMC-SS-CDR. The PEG-COOH and iRGD were further grafted on the HMC-SS-CDR. The PTX loading efficiency was quantitatively analyzed by using the HPLC method at 210 nm.

The redox and NIR dual-responsive release properties of PTX were assessed by the HPLC. The PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD samples (1 mg) were dispersed in 5 mL different media pH 6.5 PBS, pH 7.4 PBS and pH 6.5 PBS with 10 mM GSH. The free PTX in pH 6.5 PBS and pH 7.4 PBS were used as the control groups. Sample suspensions were placed in a shaker at 37 °C with a rotating speed of 100 rpm. At determined time intervals, 1 mL of the release medium was withdrawn to measure the release amount, and the same volume fresh medium was added correspondingly. For irradiation groups, the samples were illuminated by NIR laser at 1 W/cm2 for 3 min at 2 h and 6 h.

Considering the high concentration of GSH in tumor cells, the HMC-CD-iRGD was placed in pH 7.4 PBS with GSH media (0, 3, 10 mM), respectively. Then the supernatant was collected by centrifugation, and the corresponding fluorescence spectrum as well as the photothermal effect were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effects and in vitro thermal stability
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the concentration-dependent and power-dependent photothermal effects, 200 μL of serials concentrations of CDR, HMC and HMC-CD-iRGD suspensions were irradiated by an 808 nm laser. A thermal infrared imager (LaserSight, Optris, Germany) was used to record the system temperature. The samples were irradiated at 1 W/cm2 at different concentrations and different power densities at 50 μg/mL with deionized as a negative control. The photothermal stability of nanoparticles was studied by irradiating the samples, followed by cooling to room temperature without NIR irradiation for 6 cycles. The detailed calculations of the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) are based on published literatures [32,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular photothermal effect and calcein-AM/PI staining
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cell line, incubated in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% FBS and 1% streptomycin-penicillin (v/v), was used for in vitro experiments. 4T1 cells were seeded in 6-well plates surrounded by 5% CO2. After incubation for 24 h, cells were further incubated with various concentrations of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD for 2 h. Subsequently, the 4T1 cells were rinsed, collected, and resuspended in PBS, and the suspensions were irradiated at 1 W/cm2 for 3 min. In addition, 4T1 cells were seeded into 24-well plates. After incubation with PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD, the cells were irradiated at 1.0 W/cm2 for different time. Then 4T1 cells were stained with calcein-AM/PI and observed by CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and targeting ability evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: The 4T1 cellular uptake of nanoparticles was assessed through confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and flow cytometry (FCM). 4T1 cells were seeded in 24-well plates and then incubated with HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD (25 μg/mL) for 2 h. Then, the nucleus was stained with Hoechst 33258. The cell slide was observed by CLSM to visualize the cellular uptake. In addition, the FCM was used to evaluate the 4T1 cellular uptake of HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD. To assure HMC-CD-iRGD could be untaken by 4T1 cells, the 4T1 cells were incubated with 25 μg/mL HMC-CD-iRGD for 4 h, and then were fixed and observed by TEM.

The hydrophobic coumarin 6 (C6) was used to simulate PTX to evaluate the cellular uptake behavior of HMC-CD-iRGD. The preparation process of C6 loaded C6/HMC-CD-iRGD and C6/HMC-CD-PEG are the same as PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD and PTX/HMC-CD-PEG except for the usage of C6 instead of hydrophobic PTX. Briefly, 4T1 cells were cultured in a 24-well dish and incubated with free C6, C6/HMC-CD-iRGD, and C6/HMC-CD-PEG with 5 μg/mL C6. After incubation for 0.5 h, the 4T1 cells were washed with cold PBS. For the photo-thermal irradiation group, the 4T1 cells were irradiated at 1 W/cm2 for 3 min. The total incubation time was fixed at 2 h. Subsequently, the 4T1 cells were stained with Hoechst 33258 and visualized using CLSM. 4T1 cells were cultured in a 6-well plate and incubated with free C6, C6/HMC-CD-iRGD, and C6/HMC-CD-PEG (5 μg/mL C6). And the mean fluorescence intensity was measured by FCM (FACS Calibur, BD).

The cellular viability and the synergistic effect were evaluated using 4T1 cells. After overnight incubation, the cells were incubated with various concentrations of PTX, HMC-CD-iRGD or PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD for 1 d. Then, MTT solution (50 μL) was added for 4 h further incubation. The NIR irradiation groups were irradiated for 5 min (1.0 W/cm2) before the addition of MTT. The combination index (CI) was calculated based on previous works [32,37].

To evaluate the cell necrosis and apoptosis triggered by chemotherapy, PTT and combined therapy, 4T1 cells were incubated with different preparations equal to 5 μg/mL PTX. The NIR irradiation groups were irradiated at 1.0 W/cm2 for 3 min after administration for 2 h. Then, the cells were collected and stained with Annexin V-FITC/PI for FCM analysis.

The 4T1 cells were pre-incubated with different preparations with a PTX concentration of 5 μg/mL for 6 h. And an 808-nm laser was applied to irradiate at 2 h post-incubation for 3 min. Then, 4T1 cells were collected and resuspended with cold PBS, and further stained with PI for FCM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synergistic effect and penetration in multicellular tumor spheroids
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cell multicellular tumor spheroids (MCTSs) were prepared according to the published literature [38]. When the diameters of MCTSs reached approximately 400 μm, the MCTSs were incubated with normal saline, Taxol®, HMC-CD-iRGD, PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD, HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR, and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR with the corresponding PTX dose at 5 μg/mL, respectively. The PTT group was irradiated at 1 W/cm2 for 3 min. The growth of the MCTSs was observed using an optical microscope. The live and dead MCTSs were used to evaluate the synergistic effect of various preparations. After MCTSs incubation with different groups for 6 h, the MCTSs were rinsed and distinguished by calcein-AM and PI for CLSM imaging. The penetration capacity of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD was further evaluated in MCTSs model. The MCTSs were incubated with 25 μg/mL PTX/HMC-CD-PEG and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD for 6 h. The penetration and uptake of samples by MCTSs were observed using CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and tumor penetration
ce:section.ce:para: The Dir was used instead of PTX to research the in vivo performance of HMC-CD-iRGD. The female BALB/c mice (18–20 g) were provided by the Laboratory Animal Center of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University. The mice were injected subcutaneously with 5 × 106 4T1 cells. The mice were administrated with free Dir and Dir loaded HMC-CD-iRGD (Dir/HMC-CD-iRGD) containing 1 mg/kg Dir, respectively. The main organs and tumors were gathered at 2, 6 and 24 h, and the fluorescence signal intensity of Dir was observed by the IVIS system (and measured at 790 nm).

When the tumor volume of mice increased to 200 cm3, the HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD were intravenously administrated at an identical PTX dose of 5 mg/kg. After 6 h, the tumors were collected for frozen slices, and the slices were observed by CLSM after staining with Hochest 33258.

The 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were divided into 5 groups: saline group, Taxol®, PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD, HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR, and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR. All the groups were administrated with the identical PTX dose of 5 mg/kg. For the PTT group, the tumors were irradiated by NIR laser at 0.75 W/cm2 at 6 h post-injection of the preparations on the 1st, 3rd and 5th day. The tumor volumes and body weights of mice were recorded every other day. By the end, the tumors were collected and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated dUTP-biotin nick end labeling (TUNEL) staining to investigate apoptotic levels of tumor tissues. The major organs were collected and stained with H&E for histology study.

Nitrogen adsorption was carried on an adsorption analyzer (V-Sorb 2800P, Gold APP Instrument Corporation, China). The mesoporous structure of the sample was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, Eindhoven, Netherlands). Particle Size Analyzer Nicomp 380 (Particle Sizing Systems, USA) was used to verify the particle size distributions and Zeta potentials. The fluorescence spectra were characterized on a SpectraMax M5 multifunctional microplate reader (Molecular Devices, USA). The cellular imaging was observed by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Zeiss LSM 510).

CDR was synthesized facilely via the hydrothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine in DMF solvent at 200 °C. The TEM and AFM were employed to characterize the morphology and size of the CDR. As shown in TEM images (Fig. 2 A and B), CDR showed a mono-dispersed spherical morphology with a diameter of 4 nm. As shown in Fig. 2C–E, the AFM topography image and 3D image further proved that CDR had a spherical morphology with a particle height of 4.0 nm in consistent with the result of TEM. The Zeta potential of CDR was +12.6 mV due to the amine groups from p-phenylenediamine. As shown in Fig. 2F, the CDR showed the terra cotta in the sunlight and the rose red under UV light (365 nm) irradiation. And the CDR showed the excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence properties. In Fig. 2G, the CDR showed a maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 500 nm and 625 nm, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDR was 17.4% using sodium fluorescein as the reference as shown in Fig. S1. These results indicated that CDR showed red-emitting fluorescence under diverse excitation wavelengths. Then, the photothermal transduction efficiency of CDR was evaluated. Different concentrations of CDR were illuminated by 808 nm at 2 W/cm2 for 3 min with water as a blank control. (Fig. 2H and I). Under NIR irradiation, water temperature increased only less than 2 °C, while the temperature changes of CDR were related to the sample concentration. CDR (200 μg/mL) showed approximately 40 °C temperature elevation, demonstrating the eminent photothermal conversion ability. Additionally, the photostability of CDR was evaluated by 6 NIR irradiation cycles (Fig. 2J). The CDR temperature showed no distinct decrease, proving its excellent photostability. All these results demonstrated that as-prepared CDR showed excellent fluorescence properties and photo-stability, and had promising potential as NIR therapeutic agents for cancer treatment.

In this work, HMC-SS-COOH carriers were prepared according to our reported literatures [8,33]. HMC-CD-iRGD carriers were constructed by grafting the penetrable iRGD modified CDR with the excellent photothermal capability on the mesopores of HMC as the valves through disulfide bonds to realize PTT-enhanced programmed synergetic thermo-chemotherapy therapy. As shown in TEM (Fig. 3 A), the monodispersed and uniform HMC spheres with the hierarchical mesoporous hollow structure were observed. And the diameter of HMC vehicles was approximately 140 nm with a mesoporous shell of 40 nm. As shown in Fig. 3B and C, compared with the smooth surface of HMC, the surface of vehicles became rough, and the mesoporous network was blurry after the modification of CDR and PEG-linked iRGD. Additionally, the successful preparation of HMC-CD-iRGD was confirmed by diverse characterizations. The results of pore distributions, isotherms, specific surface area and total porous volume of the vehicles are shown in Fig. 3D and E and Table S1. The total porous volume and specific surface area showed a reduced trend after each modification, indicating that the gated CDR and iRGD were modified on openings of HMC-CD-iRGD. In addition, the changes of Zeta potential values provided obvious evidence for the preparation of HMC-CD-iRGD (Fig. 3F). Compared with HMC-SS-CDR, the grafting of carboxylated PEG reduced the negatively charged potential of HMC-SS-CDR. After the positively charged iRGD was modified on HMC-COOH, the Zeta potential of HMC-CD-iRGD increased to −25.8 mV. As shown in Fig. 3G, the particle sizes of HMC-COOH, HMC-SS-CDR, HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD gradually increased after each modification. Additionally, the absorbance changes and hemolysis assay were investigated to assess the dispersity stability and hemocompatibility of HMC-CD-iRGD in vivo. As shown in Fig. 3H, the normalized absorbances of HMC-COOH were obviously decreased in pH 5.0 PBS, pH 7.4 PBS and RPMI 1640 culture medium. In contrast, the normalized absorbances of HMC-CD-iRGD did not change obviously within 24 h even in the RPMI 1640 culture medium. Fig. S2 showed that the hydrodynamic diameters and PDI values of HMC-CD-iRGD nanoparticles did not change markedly in pH7.4 PBS and pH5.0 PBS, which was contributed by the electrostatic repulsion force and steric hindrance effect of outer PEG and iRGD. As displayed in Fig. 3I, the hemolysis percentage of HMC-COOH was nearly 30% at 1000 μg/mL, which could be ascribed to the strong interaction between RBCs’ membrane and HMC-COOH carriers with strong adsorption capacity and high specific area. In contrast, the hemolysis percentages of HMC-CD-iRGD nanovehicles were extremely low (2.1%) even at the high concentration of 1000 μg/mL. In addition, the CDR showed relatively low hemolysis percentage (less than 5%) at the tested concentration range due to the positively charged property as shown in Fig. S3. The improved dispersity and biocompatibility of HMC-CD-iRGD were mainly due to the steric hindrance between the grafted PEG and iRGD. These experiments proved that the HMC nanoparticles owned a huge drug loading capacity and appropriate size for targeting drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal properties of HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the UV–Vis–NIR spectra results (Fig. S4), both HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD showed strong absorption at the NIR region, which indicated their potential photothermal conversion capacities. The photothermal conversion efficiency is a vital indicator to evaluate the synergistic thermochemotherapy effect of vehicles. The characteristics of in vitro photothermal heating performance were assessed using an 808 nm laser. As shown in Fig. S5, the temperature of deionized water did not heat up after 3 min NIR irradiation. However, HMC-COOH suspension showed marked concentration-dependent and power-dependent temperature elevation properties. The temperature of 100 μg/mL HMC-COOH suspension reached 64 °C. Encouragingly, the temperature of HMC-CD-iRGD could even reach up to 78 °C under the same condition as shown in Fig. S6, indicating that HMC-CD-iRGD had a better heat-generating effect than HMC-COOH. The temperature elevation profiles of HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD suspensions under different laser intensities are shown in Figs. S7 and S8. Similarly, the temperature increment of HMC-CD-iRGD suspension under 1 W/cm2 was higher than that of HMC-COOH under the same concentration of 50 μg/mL. Additionally, photo-stability and reusable character of NIR adsorbing agents are considered to be the vital factors in thermo-chemotherapy. The photo-stability of the vehicles was evaluated by repeated NIR irradiation (Fig. 4 A and B). The photothermal effect of HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD exhibited no distinct changes after the repeated 808 nm irradiation cycles, indicating the excellent photo-stability of both HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD. To further estimate the photothermal performance of HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) values of both carriers were calculated based on published works (Fig. 4C and D) [33,34]. The η values of HMC-COOH and HMC-CD-iRGD were 28.9% and 31.3%, respectively, calculated using the sample concentration of 50 μg/mL. The above results demonstrated that the HMC-COOH and gated CDR showed a synergistic effect in photothermal properties.

The solvent evaporation method was employed to prepare PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD. The loading efficiency of PTX was 30.7% in HMC-CD-iRGD because of orderly mesoporous structure, tremendous surface area, strong adsorption capability as well as the hollow cavity of HMC carriers [32], which was much higher than the reported drug delivery systems [29,30,39]. Additionally, XRD and DSC measurements were used to evaluate the physical state of PTX in PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD. Compared with the physical mixture and raw PTX, no obvious crystalline diffraction peaks or melting point endothermic peaks were observed in PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD (Fig. 4F and G), indicating the non-crystalline state of PTX owing to the restricted mesoporous network of HMC carriers [33]. The release behaviors of raw PTX and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD in different release media are shown in Fig. 4H. The release percentages of raw PTX were less than 5% both at pH 6.5 PBS and pH 7.4 PBS due to the poorly soluble nature of PTX. PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed a relatively higher release amount at pH 6.5 PBS than that at pH 7.4 PBS. Encouragingly, the cumulative release of PTX from PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD in the presence of GSH was much faster than that of the PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD group in the same environment, which proved the reduction-responsive release behavior of HMC-CD-iRGD. Drug release results proved that HMC-CD-iRGD could increase the solubility and restrain the premature release of PTX. Additionally, the effects of 808-nm laser on PTX release were further evaluated by irradiating the suspensions at 2 h and 6 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4I, the cumulative release of PTX under irradiation exhibited a markedly accelerated release property in pH 6.5 PBS. The maximized release amount of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD was up to 55.3% in the presence of GSH and NIR irradiation at 6 h, which were 1.93-fold and 1.41-fold compared with the PTX release under GSH (28.6%) or NIR laser (39.2%). Consequently, PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed the redox/NIR double-responsive release natures, thus effectively decreasing the side effect and enhancing the antitumor therapy effect.

Furthermore, to further certify the detachment of CDR from the surface of HMC-CD-iRGD, HMC-CD-iRGD was incubated with 0, 3 or 10 mM of GSH, and the fluorescence spectra of supernatant CDR were measured. As displayed in Fig. S9, no obvious fluorescence signal was observed with 0 mM GSH. Due to the quenched property of HMC carriers, the fluorescent intensity of CDR dots was nearly disappeared after they were modified on the surface of HMC. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDR showed GSH concentration-dependent increase after they were detached from HMC-CD-iRGD. The quenching and recovery of the fluorescence signal of CDR from HMC indicated that CDR could be used to track the drug release. As shown in Fig. S10, the temperature of HMC-CD-iRGD upper supernatant in the absence of GSH did not increase obviously. In contrast, the upper supernatant of HMC-CD-iRGD incubated with 10 mM GSH had a dramatic photothermal generation effect. These results demonstrated that the iRGD modified CDR could be detached from HMC-CD-iRGD due to the fracture of disulfide bonds in the presence of GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal therapeutic effect on the cytological level
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular photothermal effects of the nanovehicles were further evaluated using 4T1 cells. As shown in Fig. 4E, the blank 4T1 cells did not heat up with NIR irradiation at 1 W/cm2 for 3 min. However, the 4T1 cells treated with HMC-CD-iRGD showed a concentration-dependent temperature elevation property. It is well accepted that the tumor cells would be ablated when the tumor temperature is higher than 42 °C [40]. The elevated temperature of 4T1 cells exceeded 15 °C after incubation with 10 μg/mL HMC-CD-iRGD, demonstrating that the tumors could be killed [41,42].

As shown in the calcein-AM/PI staining assay in Fig. 5 A, the dead cells were gradually increased (PI, red fluorescence), and the living cells (calcein-AM, green fluorescence) steadily reduced with the increase of irradiation time. Meanwhile, the green fluorescence rapidly disappeared, and evident red fluorescence was observed after the cells were irradiated for 7 min. Moreover, the living and dead cells were further identified using a trypan blue staining assay as shown in Fig. S11 [34]. The morphology of 4T1 cells changed markedly and were markedly stained blue at the high-power density of irradiation power. Therefore, HMC-CD-iRGD played an essential role in PTT treatment due to its remarkable photothermal effect and excellent capacity in tumor treatment. All these photothermal results demonstrated that the HMC-CD-iRGD might have great promise in the tumor photothermal treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of targeting, cellular drug release and penetration ability
ce:section.ce:para: To study the targeting ability of HMC-CD-iRGD, the cellular uptake of HMC-CD-iRGD and HMC-CD-PEG were compared by CLSM and FCM. As shown in Fig. 5B, the relatively weak fluorescence for the HMC-CD-PEG group was mainly located in the cell nucleus, which was attributed to the detachable positively charged CDR from HMC carriers in presence of high concentration of GSH in the cytoplasm. And a stronger red fluorescent signal from CDR was detected for the HMC-CD-iRGD group after the CDR-iRGD was separated from HMC-CD-iRGD by GSH, which proved the targeting ability of HMC-CD-iRGD at the cellular level. The quantitative cellular uptake results by FCM were consistent with the CLSM as shown in Fig. 5C. Subsequently, the bio-TEM was employed to prove intuitively the internalization of HMC-CD-iRGD after the incubation with 4T1 cells. As displayed in Fig. 5F–G and Fig. S12, lots of hollow carriers were observed in the endosomes, indicating that the HMC-CD-iRGD nanoparticles were uptaken by the 4T1 cells.

In addition, the fluorescent probe C6 was used to mimic PTX and assess the NIR irradiation on the drug release of HMC-CD-iRGD. As shown in the CLSM results in Fig. 5D, when 4T1 cells were cultured with free C6 for 2 h, no significant difference of green fluorescence was found whether NIR irradiation exists or not. The relatively weak fluorescence from CDR and C6 were observed for C6/HMC-CD-iRGD group. However, the green C6 fluorescence was markedly increased with the NIR irradiation, indicating that the local photothermal effect could accelerate drug release from HMC-CD-iRGD at the cellular level. Furthermore, the cellular release of C6/HMC-CD-iRGD was assessed by FCM (Fig. 5E). The C6 green fluorescence signal of C6/HMC-CD-iRGD under NIR irradiation was approximately 1.8-fold compared with NIR (−) group. These results proved that the local heating triggered by NIR laser could promote cellular drug release by accelerating molecular thermal motion.

To further verify the penetration of the nanoparticles, the 3D MCTSs simulating the solid tumor were employed. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the MCTSs incubated with PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed more extraordinary penetrating ability than PTX/HMC-CD-PEG. The fluorescence signal of CDR reached the interior of the MCTSs for the PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD group, whereas the CDR fluorescence was only spread on the margin for PTX/HMC-CD-PEG in the 2.5D and 3D images. The modification of iRGD improved obviously the tumor penetration ability of HMC-CD-iRGD compared with the reported ultra-small nanoparticles gated mesoporous carrier delivery systems [43–45]. Hence, these results indicated that the iRGD functional modification is beneficial for improving cellular internalization and tumor penetration ability of HMC-CD-iRGD, which is highly crucial to the PTT-enhanced synergetic therapy.

The cell apoptosis assay was used to research the chemo-photothermal mechanism of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD. As noted in Fig. 6B, for the Taxol® group, the proportion of necrotic cells and apoptotic was around 30.2%, of which the majority of cells were at the early apoptotic stage. PTX could promote the assembly of tubulin into microtubules and prevent the dissociation of microtubules, blocking cell cycle progression and mitosis, and subsequently inhibiting the growth of cancer cells [46]. The single chemotherapy group PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed higher ratios of apoptotic and necrotic cells up to 53.2%, because of the improved cellular uptake ability and increased solubility with the help of HMC-CD-iRGD. For the single PTT group, the apoptotic and necrotic cells reached around 58.8%, especially at the early apoptotic stage. This was attributed to the mitochondria, reactive oxygen species, and endoplasmic reticulum stress-related cell apoptosis activated by the hyperthermia [47,48]. Encouragingly, the combo therapy exhibited a much higher proportion (73.8%) of apoptotic and necrotic cells, which was attributed mainly to the combined hyperthermia by HMC and CDR. The enhanced hyperthermia can destroy tumor cells directly, increase the cellular uptake of nanoparticles, accelerate the release of drugs, improve the sensitivity of chemotherapeutic drugs, thus achieving the synergetic effect of the combo therapy.

Furthermore, a one-step TUNEL apoptosis assay was applied to investigate the apoptosis status of different treatment groups. After the treatment of DNase I in the positive group, the broken DNA labeled by FITC-12-dUTP showed bright green fluorescence. For the apoptotic cells, DNA endonuclease is activated and the nucleosome genomic DNA is cut off, and the DNA is immediately labeled by the green fluorescence. Therefore, the green fluorescence intensity can represent the degree of cell apoptosis. As shown in Fig. 6C, the combo treatment group exhibited brighter green fluorescence compared with the other groups, indicating that the combined group had the highest apoptosis, which was consistent with apoptosis results measured by the Annexin V-FITC apoptosis kit.

Since cell cycle arrest is related to programmed cell death, the cell cycle distribution of 4T1 cells was analyzed after different treatments using PI staining. As shown in Fig. 6D and Fig. S13, exposure of 4T1 to Taxol® increased the percentage of G2/M phase cells (38.7%) compared to the percentage of control cells (17.7%), which was due to the anti-tumor mechanism of PTX [49]. For the single chemotherapy group, the percentage of G2/M phase cells increased to 29.1%. Furthermore, the NIR group (HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR) induced a further increase in G2/M phase arrest (36.6%), which was consistent with the published literature [50]. As expected, 4T1 cells treated with PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR irradiation showed the highest G2/M phase arrest (44.3% in G2/M), accompanied by a noticeable decrease of percentage in the G0/G1 phase. The results indicated that both the anti-cancer drug PTX and NIR irradiation could block cells in the G2/M phase and inhibit their proliferation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of in vitro combinational effects
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluating the synergistic lethality of PTT and chemotherapy towards 4T1 cells, the Calcein-AM/PI co-staining assay was exploited to visualize living/dead cells and the respective proportion of living/dead cells was quantified by Image J software. As shown in Fig. 7 A and Fig. S14, the percentages of AM-stained 4T1 cells for single chemotherapy and PTT groups were 25.8% and 34.9%, respectively. In contrast, merely 1% 4T1 cells remained AM-positive for the combination therapy group.

To surmount the limitations of 2D 4T1 cells, 3D MCTSs were applied to mimic the solid tumor. As shown in Fig. 7B and C, the combined group showed higher red fluorescence intensity than any monotherapy group. Meanwhile, the PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR group had a 99.9% killing effect on 3D MCTSs observed in quantitative fluorescence analysis. Additionally, the MCTSs images were employed to further evaluate the synergistic thermo-chemotherapy. As shown in Fig. 7D, the MCTSs shrunk markedly for all treatment groups. Expectedly, in the synergistic therapy group, 3D MCTSs almost absolutely disappeared after 5 d treatment. This was because the hyperthermia triggered by laser irradiation could produce poisonous heat, accelerate the PTX release and improve the cells’ sensitivity to PTX, thus triggering the highest cytotoxicity.

The MTT assay was employed to assess the synergistic chemo-photothermal therapy of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD using 4T1 cells. As shown in Fig. 7E and F, HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD showed the high cell viability (>85%) within the detected concentration range, indicating that the HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD had good biocompatibility. Meanwhile, the IC50 values of different treatment groups are shown in Table 1 . Compared to the Taxol® group, no apparent differences in cellular activity were observed in the Taxol® + NIR group, indicating that laser irradiation was safe and biocompatible to tumor cells. For PTT groups, the cell viability of HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD gradually decreased with the increasing concentration under NIR irradiation. And the IC50 value of HMC-CD-iRGD group (4.87 μg/mL) was markedly lower than that of the HMC-CD-PEG group (8.42 μg/mL). Compared to PTX/HMC-CD-PEG group, PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed a marked decrease in cell survival under the same irradiation, further demonstrating that the iRGD ligand had excellent tumor targeting effects. As expected, the cell survival rates of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD + NIR and PTX/HMC-CD-PEG + NIR were significantly reduced compared with the chemotherapy and PTT group alone, respectively. And the PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD group showed the lowest IC50 of 0.19 μg/mL under NIR laser. The CI value is a standard indicator to prove the combined effects of two different therapies [37]. CI < 1 indicates synergism. The CI values of PTX/HMC-CD-PEG and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD were 0.60 and 0.45 as shown in Table S2, respectively. These reasons were ascribed to that the elevated temperature could damage the cells, accelerate the release of PTX and enhance the sensitivity of cancer cells to chemotherapy so as to realize chemo-photothermal synergistic antitumor treatment efficiency.

To further evaluate the target specificity of HMC-CD-iRGD towards tumor cells, the iRGD-negative normal cells (NIH-3T3 cells) were used in the in vitro cytotoxicity assay, and the related data and IC50 values are shown in Fig. S15 and Table S3. For PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD, the IC50 values increased from 0.47 μg/mL for 4T1 cells to 6.76 μg/mL for normal NIH-3T3 cells, which was attributed to that the poor cellular uptake and low release percentage of PTX reduced the efficacy of PTX for normal cells. In addition, the IC50 values of PTX/HMC-CD-PEG and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD towards NIH-3T3 cells were similar without significant difference. These results indicated that the PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD could increase the anticancer effects of PTX in tumor cells and reduce the side effects in iRGD-negative normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution, blood clearance and tumor penetration assay
ce:section.ce:para: To study the in vivo biodistribution characteristics of HMC-CD-iRGD, the fluorescence signals of Dir for free Dir and Dir/HMC-CD-iRGD groups were recorded at 2, 6, and 24 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the Dir fluorescent signal was rapidly distributed in most major organs, particularly in the liver and spleen, presumably due to the high uptake by hepatic macrophages. Compared to free Dir, the Dir/HMC-CD-iRGD group showed stronger fluorescent intensity in tumors at 24 h. And the maximal intensity of Dir/HMC-CD-iRGD appeared at 6 h because of the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect and the modified iRGD targeting ligand. The fluorescence semi-quantification results are shown in Fig. 8B. The Dir fluorescent signal of the Dir/HMC-CD-iRGD group in tumor tissue was much higher than that of the free Dir. Furthermore, the in vivo blood clearance of Cy5-labeled HMC-CD-iRGD was monitored through pharmacokinetic studies. As shown in Fig. S16 and Table S4, Cy5-labeled HMC-CD-iRGD showed long blood circulation time. And the long-time blood circulation was more conducive to the enrichment of nano-platforms at the tumor site through the EPR effect. And the concentration of Cy5-labeled HMC-CD-iRGD markedly decreased after the i.v. administration for 24 h.

Encouraged by the favorable in vitro targeting and penetration results of HMC-CD-iRGD, the in vivo tumor accumulation of HMC-CD-PEG and HMC-CD-iRGD were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 8H, the CDR fluorescence signals of the HMC-CD-iRGD group were stronger than that of HMC-CD-PEG both in the superficial tumor and the deep tumor. In addition, the red fluorescence intensity of CDR in the deep tumor was comparable with that in the superficial tumor. The above results demonstrated the excellent tumor targeting and penetration capacity of HMC-CD-iRGD after modifying the iRGD.

Based on the excitingly synergistic chemo-photothermal effect in vitro and enhanced tumor permeation and accumulation, the antitumor activity in vivo of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD under NIR laser irradiation was studied using 4T1 tumor-bearing mice model. The temperature changes of the tumor were recorded via an NIR thermal camera as shown in Fig. S17. Only moderate temperature change was observed for the saline group. In contrast, the local tumor temperature of the PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD group was markedly elevated from 31.8 °C to 56.0 °C after 3min irradiation, thus achieving the effects of ablating tumors and accelerating the drugs release. As shown in Fig. 8C, tumor growth was rapid in the saline group and was suppressed to various degrees in other groups. Tumor growth curves for PTX/HMC-CD-PEG and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD under the NIR irradiation were even decreased during the 15-day treatment. Additionally, the tumor inhibition rates were calculated as shown in Fig. 8D. The tumor inhibition rates of PTX/HMC-CD-PEG and PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD under NIR irradiation were both more than 90%, proving the more outstanding therapeutic effects of combined treatment groups than any monotherapy. Simultaneously, the combined treatment group with iRGD targeting showed the most significant inhibitory effect of 97.4%. The photographs of tumors showed the same tendency as shown in Fig. 8F. Furthermore, during the treatment period, the bodyweight of mice was recorded (Fig. 8E), and no distinct bodyweight loss was found except for the mice in the Taxol® group, indicating the apparent toxicity of Taxol®. In conclusion, the tailored PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD showed the more inspiring inhibitory effect compared with the reported work based on mesoporous carbon vehicles [33,51], which was largely ascribed to the synergistic effect of penetrating peptide modification (iRGD) and size-changeable strategy (detachable ‘meteorolite’).

Moreover, the TUNEL was performed to analyze damage of tumor tissue (Fig. 8H). The PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD or HMC-CD-iRGD with laser irradiation could cause obvious necrosis in tumor tissues. Encouragingly, the PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD with laser irradiation caused the most obvious cell apoptosis with the strongest green fluorescence among these treatments. The main organs and tumor tissues of mice in antitumor assay were subjected to histological examination to evaluate the potential toxicity of different treatment groups. The pictures of the H&E staining are shown in Fig. S18. The liver and spleen of the mice in the Taxol® group showed mild toxicity, which was manifested by the widening of cell gaps and the reduced number of nuclei. However, no noticeable pathological changes in major organs were found in the other groups. Therefore, these results illustrated that PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD had negligible side effects and an excellent antitumor effect, which held the promising potential for PTT-enhanced synergetic therapy. Additionally, in order to verify the systemic toxicity of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD, the liver functions (aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT)) and kidney functions (blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CRE)) were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S19, PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD displayed saline-comparable levels of biochemical indicators after three times treatment.

In summary, considering the advantages of HMC, the iRGD modified CDR were linked on the PTX loaded HMC for programmed tumor synergetic therapy at different depths. The CDR was gated onto HMC via disulfide bonds as the smart gatekeepers, detachable ‘meteorolite’ for photothermal ablation and the fluorescent tracker. Taking advantage of the fast redox-responsive property of disulfide bonds, PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD was stable in systemic circulation and could break into PTX/HMC and abundant CDR-iRGD rapidly after tumor accumulation. A hybrid strategy that integrated targeted/penetrated peptide functionalization and size changeable control was employed to promote the tumor penetration ability of CDR-iRGD. The HMC-CD-iRGD exhibited an increased photothermal performance (η = 31.3%) compared with HMC and a high loading capacity of 30.7% for PTX. Experiments on cytological, MCTSs' and in vivo level demonstrated the superior synergistic antitumor treatment efficiency of PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD under low-power laser irradiation compared with individual treatment. PTX/HMC-CD-iRGD exhibited an excellent tumor inhibitory effect of 97.4% with the synergistic chemo-photothermal. Given the heterogeneity and complexity of tumor tissue, this tailored nanoplatforms with detachable ‘meteorolite’ employed different therapeutic modalities to kill the tumor cells at different depths, which may provide a novel concept for programmed tumor therapy.
